content.problemStatement:
Blobo2 is in his practical exam. The teacher gave him a permutation AAA of NNN integers.

The teacher has allowed Blobo2 to make a certain type of operation on the permutation. In one operation, he can:


Apply left shift on the permutation. In other words, he can take the first element of the permutation and move it to the back of the permutation.
The teacher has asked Blobo2 to find the **lexicographically smallest** permutation possible after applying any (possibly zero) number of given operations.

Since Blobo2 wants to impress his teacher, he decided to perform **at most** two swaps in addition to the allowed operation.

Find the **lexicographically smallest** possible permutation Blobo2 can generate after applying **at most two** swaps and any number of given operations.

Note:


A permutation of size NNN consists of all integers from 111 to NNN exactly once.
During a swap, Blobo2 can choose any two indices iii and jjj (1≤i,j≤N)(1\le i,j\le N)(1≤i,j≤N) and swap AiA_iAi​ with AjA
_jAj​.
A permutation XXX is said to be lexicographically smaller than a permutation YYY if Xi<YiX_i \lt Y_iXi​<Yi​, where iii is the first index where both the permutations differ.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The second line will contain the integer NNN, denoting the size of the permutation.
The third line contains NNN distinct integers, the elements of the permutation AAA.


Output Format
Output the **lexicographically smallest** possible permutation Blobo2 can generate after applying **at most two** swaps and any number of given operations.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains an integer N, the size of the permutation. The second line contains N distinct integers A1 … AN – a permutation of {1,…,N}.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N integers – the lexicographically smallest permutation that can be obtained after any number of left‑shifts and at most two swaps.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For each test case we can rotate to bring 1 to the front and then use at most two swaps to place 2 and 3 right after it. The resulting lexicographically smallest permutation is the sorted order.
Only one element exists; it is already the smallest possible.
Rotate so that 1 is first (or swap the two elements). The sorted permutation is obtained.

solution.approach: Because we may rotate the array arbitrarily, we can always bring the element 1 to the first position without using any swap. After that we have at most two swaps left. With one swap we can place the element 2 at the second position, and with the second swap we can place the element 3 at the third position (if N ≥ 3). No lexicographically smaller element can appear earlier than 1, 2 or 3 respectively, therefore the optimal result is the fully sorted permutation 1,2,…,N. For N = 1 or 2 we only need zero or one swap. Hence the answer does not depend on the original permutation – we simply output the sorted sequence.
solution.timeComplexity: O(∑N) – we only read the input and write N numbers per test case.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        # discard permutation
        for _ in range(N):
            next(it)
        out_lines.append(' '.join(str(i) for i in range(1, N+1)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
